mnemonics.title: I PAID for Cloud Security
mnemonics.content: I = Infrastructure as a Service (Provider: Physical+Network, Customer: OS+Apps+Data); P = Platform as a Service (Provider: Physical+Network+OS+Runtime, Customer: Apps+Data); A = Applications/Software as a Service (Provider: Everything except User Data); I = Identity & Access Management (Customer responsibility in all models); D = Data (Always customer responsibility)

mnemonics.title: PUSH Deployment Models
mnemonics.content: P = Public Cloud (Shared infrastructure, owned by provider); U = Private Cloud (Dedicated infrastructure, single organization); S = Community Cloud (Shared by organizations with common concerns); H = Hybrid Cloud (Combines 2+ cloud types)

mnemonics.title: CIA Triad Always Guards Data
mnemonics.content: C = Confidentiality (Prevent unauthorized access); I = Integrity (Ensure data accuracy, no tampering); A = Availability (Data accessible when needed)

mnemonics.title: SECaaS Portfolio
mnemonics.content: SECaaS categories: Identity & Access Management, Data Loss Prevention, Web Security, Email Security, Cloud Security Posture Management, Encryption, Intrusion Detection/Prevention, Compliance Management

mnemonics.title: Zero Trust Never Trusts
mnemonics.content: Zero Trust principles: Never trust, always verify; Assume breach; Verify explicitly; Least privilege access; Micro-segmentation; Continuous monitoring

acronyms.usage: Used to continuously monitor cloud configurations, identify misconfigurations, and ensure compliance. Essential for maintaining security in dynamic cloud environments.
Used to protect cloud workloads (servers, containers, functions) from threats. Deploys agents or agentless monitoring for runtime security.
Used as a policy enforcement point between users and cloud services. Enforces security policies for data loss prevention, access control, and threat protection.
Used to manage digital identities, authenticate users, and authorize access to cloud resources. Critical for implementing least privilege principle.
Used to add extra security layer by requiring 2+ verification methods (something you know, have, or are). Highly recommended for cloud admin accounts.
Used to prevent sensitive data from leaving the organization. Monitors, controls, and blocks data transfers across cloud endpoints.
Used for centralized log collection, correlation, and threat detection across cloud infrastructure. Enables real-time security monitoring.
Used to combine network security functions with WAN capabilities. Delivers security and networking as a cloud service model.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a layered cake where the provider owns the bottom layers (physical infrastructure, hardware) and the customer owns the top layers (data, applications). The dividing line varies based on service model - IaaS has more customer responsibility, SaaS has less.

Imagine a spectrum: Public = apartment building (shared, cost-effective); Private = private bungalow (exclusive, expensive); Community = shared housing (specific group); Hybrid = connected buildings (flexible, combines benefits).

Picture a bank vault - even after entering the building, every room requires new verification. No one is trusted by default, each access point demands authentication, like castle walls with multiple checkpoint gates.

Imagine an onion with multiple protective layers - outer layer (perimeter firewall), middle layers (network segmentation, intrusion detection), inner layer (encryption, access controls). If one layer fails, others still protect.
Picture a triangle where each corner represents CIA triad. Attackers try to breach one side to reach the data inside. Security controls must protect all three sides simultaneously.

analogies.analogy: Like managing security for multiple bank branches across different cities. Each branch (cloud provider) has its own security, but you need a unified security team to monitor all branches, ensure consistent policies, and coordinate responses to threats.

Like sending a locked safe through a courier. Even if someone intercepts the courier (data in transit) or breaks into the warehouse (data at rest), they cannot access the contents without the key (decryption key).

Like dividing a large office building into separate departments with individual locked doors and access cards. Even if someone enters the building, they cannot access other departments without proper authorization.

Like a health checkup for your cloud infrastructure. Just as a doctor checks vital signs, CSPM continuously scans for configuration weaknesses, compliance gaps, and security vulnerabilities across the cloud environment.

Traditional security was like protecting a castle with walls (perimeter). In cloud, the castle has no walls - so instead, you put guards at every door and verify each person (identity) before allowing access to any room or treasure (data).
